problem_summary: 2020 MCM Problem D (Operations Research / sports analytics): given event-level passing and full-event data for a fictional "Huskies" soccer team across 38 matches of a season, build passing networks to (a) reveal team-level structural patterns plus short- and long-term dynamics, (b) define performance indicators that capture successful teamwork and test whether strategies are opponent-dependent, (c) advise the coach on changes for next season, and (d) generalize the framework to other team activities. The paper models the team as undirected weighted passing networks (nodes = players, edge weight = pass count) and constructs a composite, statistically validated teamwork Performance Indicator from three families of metrics.
models_used: Undirected weighted passing network G=(V,E,A) per match: nodes are players in the match (>11 when substitutions occur), edge weight A_ij = number of passes between i and j, symmetric adjacency matrix

Spectral/graph network metrics: largest adjacency eigenvalue lambda_1 (network strength/connectivity), algebraic connectivity lambda_2-hat = 2nd-smallest eigenvalue of Laplacian S-A (group fragmentation), weighted clustering coefficient CE_i and team-mean CE (triangle density), mean shortest-path length MSP using topological distance 1/A_ij solved via Dijkstra's algorithm

l-pass networks: sliding-window sub-networks built from l consecutive passes up to the t-th pass (e.g. 50-pass and 100-pass windows) to track short-term temporal evolution of the four structural metrics within a match

Markov Chain model for season-long (long-term) dynamics: 30 states = 30 players, state = who holds the ball, memoryless transition matrix P_ij estimated as (passes i->j)/(passes from i); stationary/stable distribution pi computed as player ball-holding probability at infinity. Explicitly reframed as a directed weighted network

Composite Performance Indicator PI = SI + CI + STI: (1) Structural Indicator SI = normalized sum of lambda_1, lambda_2-hat, CE, MSP; (2) Configurational Indicator CI = SDS + PIS + FIS (Stable Distribution Score from pi, Pass-event Involvement Score, Full-event Involvement Score), each a pass-frequency-weighted (omega_m,i) sum of per-player scores; (3) Spatial-Temporal Indicator STI = PR + OS + DS (Possession-time Ratio, Offensive Score, Defensive Score)
Possession-time estimation heuristic from fullevents.csv (consecutive same-team events -> full time delta; mixed -> half time delta) since possession time was not directly provided

Chi-squared (chi^2) independence tests on discretized indicator vs. match-outcome contingency tables (win/tie/loss) to validate that SI, CI, STI, PI correlate with results (p-values 0.03, 0.05, 0.10, 0.01)
Sensitivity analysis via 80% data subsampling and re-scoring to test robustness to missing/noisy data
key_techniques: Multi-scale modeling of the same object: one full-match static network, l-pass windowed networks for intra-match short-term dynamics, and a season-aggregate Markov chain for long-term dynamics — directly answering the prompt's 'short-term and long-term' requirement with distinct models

Min-max normalization of every raw metric across all 38 matches into [0,1] (with MSP sign-flipped so 'higher = better' holds uniformly) before summation, making heterogeneous metrics additively comparable

Hierarchical, additive indicator design (player-level scores -> match-level sub-indicators -> three indicator families -> single PI), with a clear tree diagram (Figure 6) making the construction auditable
Pass-frequency involvement weighting omega_m,i = 0.5 * (passes involving player i)/(total passes) to aggregate individual player scores into a team score that emphasizes active players

Turning the Markov stationary distribution pi into both a team metric (SDS) and a per-player importance ranking (identifying F2/M1 as star players, M7/D10 as substitutes) — reusing one model for two deliverables

Statistical validation rather than assertion: chi^2 contingency tests against actual win/tie/loss outcomes to prove indicators are 'informative,' a concrete answer to 'does this reflect teamwork success?'
Robustness check by data ablation (80% resampling) showing relative rankings persist

Qualitative pattern reading (dyadic/triadic configurations, formation geometry from average player positions) cross-checked against actual match scorelines as a sanity narrative (e.g., busy goalkeeper in match 3 -> 0:2 loss)
Conditional slicing of the PI distribution by coach (3 coaches) and by home/away side (violin plots) to translate analysis into targeted, segmented coaching advice
Grounding nearly every metric in cited sports-network literature (Guardiola's Barcelona network paper, possession-as-indicator papers, water-polo network paper) for credibility
why_it_won: "From a judge's perspective it wins on completeness, rigor, and verifiability. It answers all four sub-questions explicitly and in order, and crucially it does not stop at proposing metrics — it statistically VALIDATES them with chi^2 tests against real outcomes and stress-tests them with an 80%-data sensitivity analysis, which is exactly the 'prove your model works' evidence judges reward and most teams omit. The three-tier PI (structural / configurational / spatial-temporal) is a clean, well-motivated decomposition that maps one-to-one onto the problem's demand for 'structural, configurational, and dynamical aspects.' The short-term l-pass vs. long-term Markov-chain split is an elegant, literature-grounded way to cover both time scales with genuinely different models rather than rehashing one. The team also closes the loop to actionable advice (per-coach and home/away), and addresses the 'opponent-dependent vs. universal' sub-question with a sharp, defensible claim — that configurational score is opponent-independent (depends only on player quality) while structural and spatial-temporal scores are not. Every metric is tied to a citation, and the writing is disciplined and self-aware (honest Strengths/Weaknesses). It reads as a coherent, evidence-backed pipeline, not a grab-bag of techniques."
section_highlights: "Structure mirrors the four sub-questions and builds bottom-up: Section 3 (network construction -> qualitative patterns -> quantitative metrics -> l-pass short-term dynamics -> Markov long-term dynamics) establishes the modeling toolkit; Section 4 assembles those metrics into the validated PI; Section 5 turns PI into segmented advice; Section 6 generalizes; Section 7 gives honest strengths/weaknesses. Standout sections: (1) Section 3.2 reads dyadic/triadic configurations and formations off the network and cross-validates each interpretation against the actual scoreline (match 1 win without conceding, match 3 0:2 loss, match 4 0:4 loss) — turning network pictures into a credible narrative. (2) Section 4.4.2's chi^2 tests with explicit contingency tables and p-values is the rigor centerpiece. (3) Section 4.4.3 sensitivity analysis. (4) The Figure 6 indicator tree gives a one-glance mental model of the whole composite. The summary sheet is concrete and sequential, signposting the multi-scale modeling and the validation up front. Honest, specific Weaknesses (ignores pass direction in the undirected nets, Markov chain ignores non-pass events, no motifs/passing-zone analysis) build judge trust."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The main passing networks and l-pass networks are undirected, discarding pass direction — directionality (who initiates vs. receives) is central to attacking play; only the Markov chain captures it (self-acknowledged weakness)
The Markov chain uses only pass events and ignores shots, tackles, interceptions, and other events, so the stationary distribution is a partial picture of player influence

The composite PI is built by simple unweighted summation of min-max-normalized scores (SI = sum of 4, CI = sum of 3, STI = sum of 3); equal weighting is an unjustified choice and no weight-sensitivity (e.g., varying coefficients) is tested — only data-ablation sensitivity is done

Statistical validity is shaky: STI's chi^2 p-value is 0.10 (not significant at 0.05), and with n=38 matches split across win/tie/loss the contingency-table cells are very small, making chi^2 assumptions questionable; the paper still calls the indicators 'highly correlated'

Offensive/Defensive scores rely on raw x-coordinate averages of player positions with a hard-coded pitch length of 100 and ad hoc formulas — sensitive to coordinate conventions and not normalized per opponent style before combination
Possession time is not in the data and is reconstructed by a heuristic (split the time delta between mixed-team consecutive events) whose accuracy is unverified
Coach- and side-conditioned advice is fairly generic ('rethink strategies and formations,' 'increase possession time') and not quantitatively prescriptive

Several rendered data tables/figures in this markdown extraction appear corrupted or placeholder (e.g., repeated 0.64 MSP values, A-X filler rows, suspiciously periodic bar values), so the underlying numbers in the source PDF cannot be fully trusted from this copy
Six broad assumptions (accurate/complete data, negligible weather, fair referees, constant player fitness/cohesion) are strong and largely unrevisited in the analysis
reusable_patterns: Multi-scale modeling template: model the same system as (a) a static aggregate structure, (b) sliding-window snapshots for short-term dynamics, and (c) a Markov/stochastic-process aggregate for long-term dynamics — a clean way to satisfy any 'discuss short-term and long-term behavior' prompt with three genuinely different models

Composite-indicator recipe: pick metrics in distinct conceptual families -> min-max normalize each across all instances into [0,1] (sign-flip 'lower-is-better' metrics) -> aggregate hierarchically (entity -> sub-score -> family -> overall) -> draw a tree diagram of the hierarchy

VALIDATE your invented index against ground truth: discretize the index and the real outcome, build a contingency table, run a chi^2 test and report p-values — this converts an arbitrary-looking metric into an evidence-backed one and is a generic move for any scoring model
Robustness-by-ablation: re-run the whole scoring pipeline on a random 80% subsample and show rankings persist — a fast, generic sensitivity analysis when analytic perturbation is hard

Reuse one model for multiple deliverables: a Markov stationary distribution simultaneously yields a team-level score and a per-entity importance ranking (star vs. substitute) — extract maximum mileage from each model

Answer 'is the strategy universal or opponent-dependent?' by decomposing the score and arguing component-by-component which parts depend on the adversary (structural, spatial-temporal) vs. which are intrinsic (configurational/player quality)
Cross-validate qualitative model output against known ground-truth labels as a narrative sanity check (network pattern -> predicted result -> actual scoreline)

Conditional-distribution slicing for actionable recommendations: plot the headline metric's distribution conditioned on each decision-maker/context (per coach, home vs. away) to generate targeted advice
Reconstruct missing required quantities from available raw data with an explicit, stated heuristic (possession time from consecutive event timestamps) rather than abandoning the metric

Generalization-by-abstraction template: state the single structural requirement the method needs ('a dominant key event like a pass'), then enumerate which adjacent domains satisfy it (basketball, volleyball, co-authorship networks, company communication) and which do not (curling, gymnastics)
Anchor every modeling choice to a literature citation to build judge credibility
